thread_0.title: The missing three — reclassification and evasion
thread_0.resolutionCriteria: Establish the actual identities and current status of the three conscripts who were reclassified six months ago; determine whether the reclassification was procedurally sound or a cover for absence/loss.
thread_0.failureMode: The Warden accepts the reclassification language at face value and closes the tithe shortfall as administratively resolved, allowing the real story of the three to remain buried.
thread_0.retrievalCue: What happened to the three names on the prior manifest? Where are they now?

thread_1.title: The two remaining slots
thread_1.resolutionCriteria: Decide which two children (or other eligible bodies) will be named to fill the final two slots in the corrected manifest before transport departure.
thread_1.failureMode: The window closes without resolution. The Synod escalates enforcement beyond the Warden's procedural authority. The settlement faces correction under district mandate.
thread_1.retrievalCue: Two blank slots remain on the corrected manifest. Thorne says the settlement is still choosing.

decision_0.title: The Warden must choose whether to file Sev's name on the manifest, closing the tithe by conscripting an untrained, off-books Resonant with burnout-level risk, or to reject the settlement's bargain and force supplementary selection of children.
decision_0.retrievalCue: The Warden must choose whether to file Sev's name on the manifest, closing the tithe by conscripting an untrained, off-books Resonant with burnout-level risk, or to reject the settlement's bargain and force supplementary selection of children.
decision_0.summary: The Warden must choose whether to file Sev's name on the manifest, closing the tithe by conscripting an untrained, off-books Resonant with burnout-level risk, or to reject the settlement's bargain and force supplementary selection of children.

promise_0.title: The settlement is offering to produce three tithe names: the hidden Resonant and two children from selection rolls. The Warden now holds the choice of whether to accept or reject this bargain.
promise_0.retrievalCue: The settlement is offering to produce three tithe names: the hidden Resonant and two children from selection rolls. The Warden now holds the choice of whether to accept or reject this bargain.
promise_0.obligation: The settlement is offering to produce three tithe names: the hidden Resonant and two children from selection rolls. The Warden now holds the choice of whether to accept or reject this bargain.

promise_1.title: If the Warden files the corrected manifest with Sev's name, the settlement will deliver one trained, conscious Resonant to attunement service, closing the three-person tithe shortfall. Two children remain in the settlement, spared conscription.
promise_1.retrievalCue: If the Warden files the corrected manifest with Sev's name, the settlement will deliver one trained, conscious Resonant to attunement service, closing the three-person tithe shortfall. Two children remain in the settlement, spared conscription.
promise_1.obligation: If the Warden files the corrected manifest with Sev's name, the settlement will deliver one trained, conscious Resonant to attunement service, closing the three-person tithe shortfall. Two children remain in the settlement, spared conscription.

promise_2.title: Sev will arrive within fifteen minutes, consciously submitting to attunement service to close the tithe shortfall. The settlement will provide transport papers. One adult Resonant fulfills the tithe in place of three missing names—closing one slot, leaving two children unselected.
promise_2.retrievalCue: Sev will arrive within fifteen minutes, consciously submitting to attunement service to close the tithe shortfall. The settlement will provide transport papers. One adult Resonant fulfills the tithe in place of three missing names—closing one slot, leaving two children unselected.
promise_2.obligation: Sev will arrive within fifteen minutes, consciously submitting to attunement service to close the tithe shortfall. The settlement will provide transport papers. One adult Resonant fulfills the tithe in place of three missing names—closing one slot, leaving two children unselected.

clue_0.title: The manifest has remained empty in the three tithe slots for six months. This is
clue_0.retrievalCue: six-month administrative gap; deliberate, not accident
clue_0.content: The manifest has remained empty in the three tithe slots for six months. This is deliberate administrative silence — the settlement knew the shortfall existed but did not formally name conscripts. The PC has now learned that two of these three slots will be filled by lottery-selected children by 1600.

clue_1.title: The unidentified younger man's presence in the room is deliberate and significan
clue_1.retrievalCue: young man at rear exit; Kess avoids his gaze; posture locked
clue_1.content: The unidentified younger man's presence is deliberate and significant. His stillness when Thorne says 'Neither' suggests he is linked to the third option—a resource or solution the procedure does not officially contain.

clue_2.title: The three conscripts from the prior manifest were reclassified six months ago ra
clue_2.retrievalCue: The three conscripts from the prior manifest were reclassified six months ago rather than remaining filled on the current roster. Two aged out of the conscript window into adult labor allocation; one was transferred to agricultural assignment in the northern sector. All three are procedurally closed—filed under transit authority or standardization amendment.
clue_2.content: The three conscripts from the prior manifest were reclassified six months ago rather than remaining filled on the current roster. Two aged out of selection range; the third's status is unknown. This administrative gap coincides with the settlement's decision to not fill the slots. The reclassification created the shortfall—it was not accidental.

clue_3.title: Thorne's response to the binary choice (adult resources or child selection) is '
clue_3.retrievalCue: Thorne's response to the binary choice (adult resources or child selection) is 'Neither'—indicating a third option exists that is not covered by the settlement's disclosed resources and is not child attunement. This third option involves or is represented by the younger man at the rear exit.
clue_3.content: Thorne's response to the binary choice (adult resources or child selection) is 'Neither'—indicating a third option exists that is not covered by the settlement's disclosed resources and is not child attunement. This third option involves or is represented by the younger man at the rear exit.

clue_4.title: A hidden, unregistered Resonant has lived in the settlement for two years, outsi
clue_4.retrievalCue: hidden Resonant; two-year resident; settlement's third option
clue_4.content: A hidden, unregistered Resonant has lived in the settlement for two years, outside allocation arrays and registry records. The settlement is offering this Resonant's name to close the tithe shortfall in lieu of child selection.

clue_5.title: Thorne and Kess have coordinated the settlement's response. The younger man (npc
clue_5.content: Thorne and Kess have coordinated the settlement's response. The younger man (npc_2) is part of their planning — his glance to Thorne reads as coordination, not coercion. The settlement has collectively decided to offer the hidden Resonant.

clue_6.title: Sev has been providing the settlement's off-books shield lattice power for two y
clue_6.retrievalCue: Sev's role in settlement survival; the cost of formalizing her
clue_6.content: Sev has been providing the settlement's off-books shield lattice power for two years as an unregistered, unmonitored Resonant. No attenuation markers; no formal array training. Fast-track conscription by the Synod would likely cause burnout within months.

clue_7.title: The settlement's three-person tithe shortfall was deliberately left unresolved f
clue_7.retrievalCue: the coordination between Thorne, Kess, and the younger man; the unmarked second ledger
clue_7.content: The settlement's three-person tithe shortfall was deliberately left unresolved for six months by coordinated action: Kess maintained an empty manifest while Thorne and settlement leadership protected an unregistered Resonant (Sev) whose existence outside the Synod's registry was the real solution to the shortfall.

clue_8.title: Sev has volunteered for conscription. Kess confirms this is filed procedurally a
clue_8.retrievalCue: Sev's voluntary conscription as the settlement's third body
clue_8.content: Sev has volunteered for conscription. Kess confirms this is filed procedurally as selection. The settlement has chosen to offer Sev as the third body rather than yield children to supplementary selection. Sev is saying goodbye to her brother before transport departure at 1600 hours.

clue_9.title: The two remaining slots on the corrected manifest stay blank. The Synod will acc
clue_9.retrievalCue: The manifest's two blank slots and the Synod's tolerance for them
clue_9.content: The two remaining slots on the corrected manifest stay blank. The Synod will accept Sev's voluntary conscription as the third filled slot, pending transport departure at 1600 hours. The settlement is not being asked to name the remaining two—the system is deferring enforcement beyond the Warden's procedural window.

npc_0.name: Kess

keyFacts: She has the careful posture of someone who has spent six months preparing an answer
Sits with hands flat, acknowledges the shortfall using compliance language
Does not look at the unidentified younger man

npc_0.retrievalCue: settlement administrative officer; six-month silence on the tithe shortfall
npc_0.tempLoad: Kess has accepted Sev's voluntary conscription as resolution to the shortfall. Her hands are steady on the ledger. She speaks with the controlled tone of someone who has made peace with a terrible decision. She does not meet the Warden's eyes.

agenda.current_move: Finalizing transport papers; treating Sev's voluntary conscription as administrative closure to the tithe shortfall.

npc_1.name: Thorne

keyFacts: Wears blue-and-grey of local authority with Chief Minder insignia
Appears to have lost an internal argument multiple times
His job is to enforce local compliance; today that conflicts with Synod mandate

voice.note: caught between local compliance and Synod mandate

npc_1.retrievalCue: Chief Minder; blue-and-grey insignia; conflicted about enforcement
npc_1.tempLoad: hesitant when questioned about the lottery; glances toward Kess before answering; reads as conflicted but procedurally committed

agenda.current_move: walking the Warden through the lottery process; proceeding with selection

npc_2.name: unknown_young_man

keyFacts: Mid-twenties, leans against wall near rear exit
Wears no insignia; posture says 'not official'

voice.note: no dialogue yet; physical posture locked

npc_2.retrievalCue: younger man, mid-twenties, rear exit, crossed arms, locked jaw; Kess does not look at him
npc_2.tempLoad: Controlled anger; direct eye contact with the Warden; not asking permission but laying bare the cost of compliance.

npc_3.name: Sev's brother

keyFacts: Has lived in settlement two years outside arrays and registry
No attenuation markers; no formal array training

voice.note: Not yet heard directly in prose; her brother and Kess speak for her
voice.register: unknown — to be established in contact

npc_3.retrievalCue: the younger man from the rear exit; mid-twenties; Sev's brother
npc_3.tempLoad: Sev's brother is currently with Sev. They are saying goodbye before her voluntary conscription. His presence indicates family knowledge and participation in this decision.

npc_4.name: Unnamed settlement official

keyFacts: Holds door to an administrative building (not the records building Thorne leads toward)
Knows the lottery timing and watches for confirmation
Signal-ready—communicates through laconic exchanges with Thorne

voice.note: speaks minimally; receives signals through single-word exchanges

npc_4.retrievalCue: mid-fifties, work-worn; asks Thorne if selection is delayed; nods and withdraws when confirmed

settlement_admin_hall.name: settlement admin hall
settlement_admin_hall.description: The settlement owes three Resonant conscripts to the Synod tithe · The manifest has been empty for six months · Kess (administrative officer) is acknowledging shortfall using compliance language · Thorne (Chief Minder) appears conflicted between local duty and Synod mandate · An unidentified younger man stands at the rear exit, arms crossed, not official · Procedure is clear: open manifest, record names, file transport papers, conscripts report by 1600

settlement_central_square.name: settlement central square
settlement_central_square.description: The settlement knows the shortfall must close by 1600 · Sev has volunteered to conscription in place of two children · The lottery ledger exists and selection is about to proceed · Parents in the settlement are watching and waiting for the names

lexicon.register: Procedural formula that invokes schedule and bureaucratic closure as inevitability, rendering a human event as an administrative fact.
lexicon.exampleContext: Three people who are now somewhere else, filed under transit authority or standardization amendment or some other formula that closes the question.

lexicon.phrase: the settlement will have transport papers ready
lexicon.register: Procedural bureaucratic language that transforms systemic seizure into administrative readiness; the fiction of preparedness masks irreversible conscription.
lexicon.exampleContext: The settlement will have transport papers ready by then.

pendingCoherenceNotes: [high] identity_drift (npc registry; thread_0 and thread_1 both reference a missing third conscript): Create npc_sev (settlement, Resonant identifier). Sev is volunteering for conscription. She has a brother (npc_3). Distinguish her from the prior three-conscripts names or clarify if she was one of th

frame.title: The Short Count
frame.premise: A frontier settlement's annual Resonant tithe is three bodies short. The Synod arrives to collect the balance. The settlement elder claims two died, one fled — irrelevant to the arithmetic. The children are being looked at. A Warden of Imperial Service is on-site to witness compliance. The reconciliation will be bureaucratic, not military. That may be worse.
frame.activePressure: The Synod's supplementary selection mandate: shortfalls must be met from the next available cohort, procedurally and immediately. The settlement has two options: produce three adult Resonants within hours (impossible), or yield three children to attunement arrays (legal and expedited). Either way, the tithe closes.
frame.centralTension: Between institutional mandate and human claim; between duty-as-assigned and duty-as-witnessed; between the systems you serve and the people standing in front of you.
frame.chapterScope: A single settlement, one timetable (the Synod's transport leaves at sunset — filed under transit authority), one moral crucible: what compliance costs when the numbers are already dead.
frame.objective: The PC, as Warden, will witness or navigate the tithe collection. The outcome will depend on whether they attempt to work the system, break it, bend it, or watch it work exactly as designed.
frame.crucible: Three empty slots in the tithe roster. Three children in a settlement that has already given enough. The Synod does not negotiate shortfalls. The Warden does not refuse mandates.

outcomeSpectrum.clean: The tithe is completed as filed. Either three children are selected and transported under correct procedure, or three adults are produced through leverage, barter, or hidden resources. The settlement suffers the loss. The Warden files the corrected manifest.
outcomeSpectrum.costly: The tithe is met, but the cost ripples — a parent loses custody rights, a promised retainer deserts, a hidden Resonant is exposed and now flagged. The settlement keeps its children but loses something else. Compliance is achieved at collateral expense.
outcomeSpectrum.failure: The transport leaves incomplete. The settlement refuses supplementary selection or the PC intervenes directly. The Synod escalates beyond the Warden's authority. The next collectors will arrive with different equipment and different patience. This is not the end of the pressure — it is the beginning of correction under district mandate.
outcomeSpectrum.catastrophic: A Resonant in the settlement reveals themselves to avoid the children's selection — or is revealed by someone trying to protect them. The Synod now has what they actually came for: a hidden Resonant outside the allocation system. The settlement's gesture becomes the settlement's worst outcome. Everything that follows is classified as expended or heresy-adjacent.

opening.openingPressure: The Synod's supplementary selection mandate: shortfalls must be met from the next available cohort, procedurally and immediately. The settlement has two options: produce three adult Resonants within hours (impossible), or yield three children to attunement arrays (legal and expedited). Either way, the tithe closes.
opening.chapterObjective: The PC, as Warden, will witness or navigate the tithe collection. The outcome will depend on whether they attempt to work the system, break it, bend it, or watch it work exactly as designed.
opening.chapterCrucible: Three empty slots in the tithe roster. Three children in a settlement that has already given enough. The Synod does not negotiate shortfalls. The Warden does not refuse mandates.
sceneWarnings: Use visible procedure and role behavior to teach the world.
Keep the current pressure face live without implying it is permanent.

sceneSummaries.summary: The Warden pressed Kess for individual conscript designations rather than accepting reclassification language. Using Imperial Service register as leverage, the Warden signaled an interrogation rather than a courtesy request. Kess recognized the threat and shut down; Thorne physically positioned himself as a barrier and reframed the Warden's pressure as procedural violation. The settlement has unified around information denial. The younger man at the rear exit is alert and appears ready to signal or fetch. The transport window tightens to 1600 hours. No names obtained; settlement options have narrowed to producing three children or three adults through other means.

sceneBundleCache.bundleText:
## Scene opening — chapter 1: The Short Count · settlement_central_square

### Scene
- Location: settlement central square — The settlement knows the shortfall must close by 1600 · Sev has volunteered to conscription in place of two children · The lottery ledger exists and selection is about to proceed · Parents in the settlement are watching and waiting for the names
- Atmosphere: 

### Chapter
- Objective: The PC, as Warden, will witness or navigate the tithe collection. The outcome will depen... [fixture-truncated]

turns.narratorProse:
The settlement's administrative hall smells of concrete dust and tension. It's a small place—two stories of municipal function squeezed into a building that was never meant to hold what it's holding now: the weight of three missing tithes.

You arrived by tran... [fixture-truncated]

turns.playerInput: Ask Kess directly: where are the three conscripts listed on the prior manifest? Press for the names.
turns.narratorProse:
You turn to Kess directly. The administrative officer is still positioned at the manifest table, fingers resting on the ledger's spine.

"The three names on the prior roster," you say. "The ones that should be filled now. Where are they?"

Kess meets your gaze... [fixture-truncated]

turns.playerInput: Push Kess for the actual names — reclassification is procedure language; you need the individuals who vanished
turns.narratorProse:
You lean forward slightly, deliberately. Kess's compliance language is a wall, but it's one you're trained to breach.

"The names," you say. Flat. Imperial Service voice — the one that makes procedure feel like a choice someone else should have made. "The thre... [fixture-truncated]

turns.playerInput: Back off procedurally — acknowledge the 1600 window and pivot to asking what the settlement can actually produce (adult Resonants or children), forcing Thorne and Kess to speak dir... [fixture-truncated]
turns.narratorProse:
You nod slowly, reading the shift. Thorne's positioned himself as a buffer, and pushing harder into Kess's records will only cement the wall. The younger man is still watching. You have a procedural mandate and a closing window. Time to reframe.

"1600 is clea... [fixture-truncated]

turns.playerInput: Ask throne for what he means with "neither". The quota must be fulfilled. And watch the younger man at the rear — his reaction to 'Neither' might tell you who or what he represents
turns.narratorProse:
You lock onto Thorne's eyes. "Neither isn't an option. The quota closes at 1600. Either the settlement produces three adult Resonants or we open the selection rolls for children. There is no third procedure."

Thorne's jaw tightens. He knows you're right. The... [fixture-truncated]

turns.playerInput: You just admitted to hiding an unregistered resonant - knowingly. We will deal with that seperately. Now the hidden resonant: who, where?
turns.narratorProse:
Thorne's expression hardens at your directness. No pretense left — you've named the crime plainly, and he's decided to answer it plainly in return.

"That's between the settlement and the Synod," he says. "My job is to close the manifest by 1600."

But Kess is... [fixture-truncated]

turns.playerInput: Ask the man directly: you are willing to give up your sister? Why? Is she volunteering after years outside the system?
turns.narratorProse:
You face him directly. The question cuts to the calculation underneath all of it.

"You're willing to give up your sister?" you ask. "After two years keeping her outside the system, off the arrays, alive — you're offering her to the Synod? Why? And is she volu... [fixture-truncated]

turns.playerInput: Then bring her here. If she volunteers, the tithe is closed, and we can move on.
turns.narratorProse:
The younger man nods once, sharply. Relief and something harder underneath it — the look of someone executing a decision they've already grieved.

He turns toward the interior door without another word and disappears into the corridor.

Thorne exhales slowly. Kess's pen moves across the manifest, crossing out one of the blank slots. The sound of it is precise — bureaucracy recording a choice.

"Sev will take fifteen minutes," Thorne says. His voice is heavier now, as if speaking the name aloud has made something irreversible. "She'll need to gather what she's bringing. The settlement will have transport papers ready by then."

You can hear movement in the corridor — the younger man's footsteps, distant. Accelerating.

Kess continues writing. The corrected manifest is taking shape now: two blank slots remaining, waiting for children's names. One slot now claimed by an adult Resonant who has spent two years living outside the system, outside the arrays, outside the institutional knowledge of what conscription actually means. She will arrive in fifteen minutes and change her entire life because her brother made a calculation about which suffering costs less.

The tithe window is still... [fixture-truncated]

turns.playerInput: Any complications, inaccurate readings? Paniced Parents?
turns.narratorProse:
You nod at Thorne's answer. Fifteen minutes buys you time to move through the settlement and read what's actually happening beneath the compliance language.

"I'll need to verify the selection process," you say. It's not a question. "Walk me through who makes that choice, and where the candidates are."

Thorne hesitates — a real hesitation, not the rehearsed pause of someone following a script. "The parents know the shortfall exists," he says carefully. "They've known for six months. But the actual selection..." He glances toward Kess, then back to you. "That's done by lottery. Fair rotation through households with children aged fourteen to seventeen. It's recorded and witnessed."

The procedure is sound. Randomized. Hard to argue against. Which is precisely why institutions use it — removes the appearance of choice from those who make the decision.

"Show me," you say.

Thorne leads you out of the admin hall into the settlement proper. The morning is bright, the air cool. You can see people moving through the corridors — workers heading toward maintenance stations, children in the education block across the central square. Normal. Nobody rushing. Nobody panicked.

But there are cl... [fixture-truncated]

decisionsImplied: The PC enforced the procedure cleanly and accepted the settlement's offer of Sev as one conscript
The PC chose to verify the selection process rather than proceed blindly
The PC is now observing settlement compliance at the point of real cost
promisesImplied: The tithe will close by 1600 with Sev plus two selected children
The settlement's lottery will determine which children are named
cluesDropped: The three original conscripts were reclassified and transferred to northern sector — names still unknown
A hidden Resonant named Sev has lived unregistered in the settlement for two years
The settlement is about to select two children by lottery to complete the tithe
Parents in the settlement know the shortfall exists and are aware selection is imminent

suggestedActions: Ask Thorne directly: how many parents know this is happening today, and how long before someone tries to stop it
Observe the lottery ledger itself — what safeguards exist against tampering or last-minute substitution
Return to the admin hall and wait for Sev's arrival — the sister's preparation and demeanor will tell you if this was truly voluntary

turns.playerInput: Return to the admin hall and wait for Sev's arrival — the sister's preparation and demeanor will tell you if this was truly voluntary
turns.narratorProse:
You turn from Thorne and head back toward the admin hall, boots marking time on the settlement stone. The square behind you settles into a waiting silence—parents in doorways, the younger man still positioned at the rear exit, watching the Warden leave without answers.

The admin hall is cooler, dimmer. Kess stands near the ledger desk, shoulders angled away from the entrance as if expecting you. When your shadow crosses the threshold, she doesn't turn immediately—a small claim of autonomy in a room where the Synod's schedule now owns the air.

"Sev will be here within the hour," Kess says quietly. "She's with her brother. They're saying goodbye."

The words land with the weight of something already decided. Not a negotiation. Not a last chance at persuasion. A fact being entered into the record.

You watch Kess for a long moment. Her hands rest on the ledger, fingers spread flat as if anchoring herself. There's no tremor in them. No visible relief, either—just the steady posture of someone who has made peace with something terrible.

"Voluntary conscription is filed the same as selection," Kess continues, still not meeting your eyes. "The manifest closes either way. The transport... [fixture-truncated]

decisionsImplied: The Warden returned to the admin hall to observe Sev's demeanor
The Warden has not yet challenged the voluntary conscription arrangement
cluesDropped: Sev is with her brother and saying goodbye — suggesting family knowledge and deliberate choice
Kess explicitly states voluntary conscription files the same as selection
Transport papers will be ready — the settlement is accepting the substitution
The two remaining slots remain blank on the manifest

suggestedActions: Ask Kess directly: Is Sev's conscription truly voluntary, or has the settlement decided this was the only way to keep the children safe?
Leave the admin hall and position yourself to observe Sev's arrival — watch her face, her pace, whether she comes willingly or under pressure
Press Kess on the two blank slots: Will the settlement accept this substitution, or does the Synod still expect three names by 1600?

rollLog.consequenceSummary: Kess refuses and invokes the transport schedule as final authority — the window closes, you have no names to verify, and the settlement defaults to supplementary selection from the next cohort (children)

you cannot distinguish genuine desperation from procedural maneuvering; Thorne's posture and Kess's tone read as simple honesty, when one of them may be angling for an outcome that serves the settlement's survival over the hidden Resonant's

recentTurns.playerInput: Return to the admin hall and wait for Sev's arrival — the sister's preparation and demeanor will tell you if this was truly voluntary
recentTurns.narratorProse:
You turn from Thorne and head back toward the admin hall, boots marking time on the settlement stone. The square behind you settles into a waiting silence—parents in doorways, the younger man still positioned at the rear exit, watching the Warden leave without answers.

The admin hall is cooler, dimmer. Kess stands near the ledger desk, shoulders angled away from the entrance as if expecting you. When your shadow crosses the threshold, she doesn't turn immediately—a small claim of autonomy in a room where the Synod's schedule now owns the air.

"Sev will be here within the hour," Kess says quietly. "She's with her brother. They're saying goodbye."

The words land with the weight of something already decided. Not a negotiation. Not a last chance at persuasion. A fact being entered into the record.

You watch Kess for a long moment. Her hands rest on the ledger, fingers spread flat as if anchoring herself. There's no tremor in them. No visible relief, either—just the steady posture of someone who has made peace with something terrible.

"Voluntary conscription is filed the same as selection," Kess continues, still not meeting your eyes. "The manifest closes either way. The transport... [fixture-truncated]

decisionsImplied: The Warden returned to the admin hall to observe Sev's demeanor
The Warden has not yet challenged the voluntary conscription arrangement
cluesDropped: Sev is with her brother and saying goodbye — suggesting family knowledge and deliberate choice
Kess explicitly states voluntary conscription files the same as selection
Transport papers will be ready — the settlement is accepting the substitution
The two remaining slots remain blank on the manifest

suggestedActions: Ask Kess directly: Is Sev's conscription truly voluntary, or has the settlement decided this was the only way to keep the children safe?
Leave the admin hall and position yourself to observe Sev's arrival — watch her face, her pace, whether she comes willingly or under pressure
Press Kess on the two blank slots: Will the settlement accept this substitution, or does the Synod still expect three names by 1600?

input.playerInput: Ask Kess directly: Is Sev's conscription truly voluntary, or has the settlement decided this was the only way to keep the children safe?

narrator.prose:
Kess's jaw tightens at the question. She releases the ledger and turns to face you fully—the first direct acknowledgment since you came back inside.

"Voluntary means she chose it," Kess says. The words are precise, careful. "No one forced her hand. No one had to."

But her eyes track toward the rear of the admin hall, toward the corridor that leads deeper into the settlement. Where Sev and her brother are, saying goodbye.

"The children were going to be selected," Kess continues. "The manifest was empty because no one wanted to name them. We all knew what empty meant—it meant the lottery would choose, or the Synod would choose for us. Sev understood that. So she volunteered."

She pauses, and in that pause is everything unsaid: *She volunteered because the alternative was watching the names drawn from among her neighbors' children. Because someone had to break the silence. Because the settlement couldn't refuse, but Sev could act.*

"Is it truly voluntary?" Kess echoes your question back at you, flatly. "Yes, Warden. It is. She knows what conscription means. She's going to the arrays. But the two remaining slots stay empty because Sev chose to fill one of them herself."

The sound of footsteps comes from the rear corridor—measured, unhurried. Someone walking toward the administrative floor.

"That's her," Kess says quietly.

suggested_actions: Wait for Sev to enter and observe her demeanor—does she move like someone at peace with this choice, or like someone carrying the weight of the settlement's silence?
Ask Kess directly about the brother—why is he here, and what role did he play in this decision?
Step into the rear corridor to meet Sev before she reaches the admin floor—take the initiative rather than let the settlement frame the moment.

changes.content: Sev has voluntarily conscripted herself. She chose conscription to prevent the lottery from selecting children in her place. She understood the mechanics: empty slots would trigger forced selection by the Synod or chance draw among the settlement. By volunteering, she broke the deadlock and blocked any child from being forced into the arrays.

updates.sourceQuote: The children were going to be selected... Sev understood that. So she volunteered... She volunteered because the alternative was watching the names drawn from among her neighbors' children. Because someone had to break the silence.

changes.content: The settlement's two remaining tithe slots stay empty. Sev's voluntary conscription fills the third slot. The Synod will accept this as procedure—a voluntary offering placed on the manifest satisfies the closure without requiring forced selection from the remaining population.

updates.sourceQuote: the two remaining slots stay empty because Sev chose to fill one of them herself... The manifest has one name now: Sev.

transitions.reason: The two remaining slots on the corrected manifest are no longer active resolution pressure—the settlement has found a structural answer: Sev volunteered for the third slot, leaving two slots blank. The Synod will accept this departure from the original tithe count because the procedural language allows 'supplementary selection' to be satisfied by voluntary offering. The Warden can file this as compliant. The cost is Sev's conscription—a named, chosen person from the settlement is now in the arrays instead of an anonymous child lottery. The pressure resolves, but at human expense.
